questions.question:
Which one of the following prefixes exactly represents the range of IP addresses 10.12.0.0 to 10.12.3.255?
A. 10.12.0.0/23
B. 10.12.0.0/22
C. 10.12.0.0/21
D. 10.12.0.0/24
questions.to_find: The CIDR prefix that exactly covers the range 10.12.0.0 to 10.12.3.255

understand.plain: We need to find a single CIDR block that starts at 10.12.0.0 and ends exactly at 10.12.3.255. A CIDR prefix /n means the first n bits are fixed and the remaining (32-n) bits can vary. The block must not include any address outside the given range.

keywords.explain: A method for allocating IP addresses using a prefix length that specifies how many bits are the network part.
The number of fixed bits in the network portion. The host portion has (32-n) bits, giving 2^(32-n) addresses.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='520' height='160' viewBox='0 0 520 160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='500' height='40' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='36' class='lbl ink'>10.12.0.0/22 : 00001010.00001100.000000xx.xxxxxxxx</text><rect x='10' y='60' width='500' height='30' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='20' y='81' class='tag ink'>Fixed 22 bits: 10.12.0-3.x  |  Host bits: 10 bits = 1024 addresses</text><rect x='10' y='100' width='240' height='30' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='121' class='sub ink'>Start: 10.12.0.0</text><rect x='270' y='100' width='240' height='30' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='280' y='121' class='sub ink'>End:   10.12.3.255</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The 22-bit prefix fixes the first three octets' top bits, leaving 10 host bits. The third octet varies from 0 to 3 (2 bits), and the fourth octet varies from 0 to 255 (8 bits), giving exactly 10.12.0.0 to 10.12.3.255.

given.aim: Find the CIDR prefix that exactly matches the address range 10.12.0.0 to 10.12.3.255.

terms.connects: The network address (all host bits = 0) of the CIDR block.
The broadcast address (all host bits = 1) of the CIDR block.

terms.meaning: Total IPs in range = End - Start + 1
terms.connects: With 2^10 addresses the host portion is 10 bits, so prefix = 32 - 10 = /22.

given.plan: Count the total addresses in the range (1024 = 2^10). Since host bits = 10, the prefix length = 32 - 10 = 22. Verify 10.12.0.0 is a valid /22 boundary (last two octets of the network address should be 0 when masked to /22).

steps.title: Compute prefix length
steps.apply: prefix_length = 32 - 10 = 22. So the prefix is /22. The network address 10.12.0.0 in binary ends with ...00001100.00 | 000000.00000000, confirming the 22-bit boundary is correct.
steps.note: Check /23: covers only 512 addresses (10.12.0.0 to 10.12.1.255) — too small. /21 covers 2048 addresses (10.12.0.0 to 10.12.7.255) — too large.

solution.result: The prefix 10.12.0.0/22 exactly represents the range 10.12.0.0 to 10.12.3.255. Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: CIDR block size = 2^(32 - prefix_length); the start address must align to the block boundary (all host bits = 0).

questions.question:
A machine receives an IPv4 datagram. The protocol field of the IP header has the protocol number of a protocol X. Which ONE of the following is NOT a possible candidate for X?
A. Internet Control Message Protocol (ICMP)
B. Internet Group Management Protocol (IGMP)
C. Open Shortest Path First (OSPF)
D. Routing Information Protocol (RIP)
questions.to_find: Which protocol cannot appear as the value of the IPv4 Protocol header field

understand.plain: The IPv4 Protocol field identifies what is encapsulated in the IP datagram's payload. Some protocols are carried directly over IP (ICMP, IGMP, OSPF), while RIP is an application-layer routing protocol that runs over UDP, so it is never directly encapsulated in IP with its own protocol number.

keywords.explain: An 8-bit field in the IP header that identifies the higher-layer protocol carried in the payload (e.g., 1=ICMP, 2=IGMP, 6=TCP, 17=UDP, 89=OSPF).
keywords.example: A TCP segment inside an IP packet has Protocol = 6.

keywords.explain: A distance-vector routing protocol that runs over UDP port 520. It is NOT directly encapsulated in IP; instead it is payload of a UDP datagram.
keywords.example: RIP packet -> UDP -> IP, so IPv4 Protocol field = 17 (UDP), not a RIP-specific number.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='480' height='180' viewBox='0 0 480 180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='200' height='40' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='110' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>ICMP  (proto=1)</text><rect x='10' y='60' width='200' height='40' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='110' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>IGMP  (proto=2)</text><rect x='10' y='110' width='200' height='40' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='110' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>OSPF  (proto=89)</text><rect x='260' y='10' width='210' height='140' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='365' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>RIP</text><text x='365' y='60' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>over UDP (port 520)</text><text x='365' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>IP Protocol field = 17</text><text x='365' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>(NOT directly in IP)</text><text x='20' y='170' class='sub mut'>Left: direct IP payload | Right: RIP is inside UDP</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: ICMP, IGMP, and OSPF are directly encapsulated in IP with their own protocol numbers. RIP runs over UDP (port 520), so the IPv4 Protocol field shows 17 (UDP), not a RIP-specific number.

given.aim: Identify the protocol that is NOT directly encapsulated in an IPv4 datagram (i.e., has no assigned IP Protocol number as a direct payload).

terms.meaning: 1 — Internet Control Message Protocol, directly in IP
terms.example: ping uses ICMP; IP Protocol field = 1.
terms.connects: ICMP is a valid candidate for protocol X.

terms.meaning: 2 — Internet Group Management Protocol, directly in IP
terms.example: Multicast group joins use IGMP; IP Protocol field = 2.
terms.connects: IGMP is a valid candidate for protocol X.

terms.meaning: 89 — Open Shortest Path First, directly in IP
terms.example: OSPF hello packets have IP Protocol field = 89.
terms.connects: OSPF is a valid candidate for protocol X.

terms.meaning: RIP uses UDP as its transport; no IP Protocol number assigned to RIP directly
terms.connects: RIP cannot appear as protocol X in the IP Protocol field.

given.plan: Check IANA-assigned IP protocol numbers. ICMP=1, IGMP=2, OSPF=89 are all directly carried in IP. RIP is an application-layer protocol over UDP and has no direct IP protocol number, so it is NOT a valid candidate.

steps.title: Verify ICMP, IGMP, OSPF as direct IP protocols
steps.apply: ICMP (RFC 792): IP Protocol = 1. IGMP (RFC 1112): IP Protocol = 2. OSPF (RFC 2328): IP Protocol = 89. All three are directly encapsulated in IP and would correctly appear in the Protocol field.
steps.note: These protocols all use IP as their direct transport; no UDP/TCP layer is between them and IP.

steps.title: Check RIP's transport
steps.apply: RIP (RFC 1058 / RFC 2453) is a UDP-based protocol (port 520). When a router sends a RIP message, the encapsulation is: RIP payload -> UDP header -> IP header. The IP Protocol field = 17 (UDP). There is no direct IP Protocol number assigned to RIP.
steps.note: Because RIP sits above UDP, the IPv4 Protocol field will always read 17, never a 'RIP' value. Hence RIP is NOT a possible candidate for X.

solution.result: RIP is NOT a possible candidate for protocol X because it runs over UDP, not directly over IP. Answer: D.

questions.formula_note: IANA protocol numbers: ICMP=1, IGMP=2, TCP=6, UDP=17, OSPF=89. RIP has no direct IP protocol number — it uses UDP port 520.

questions.question:
For which one of the following reasons does Internet Protocol (IP) use the Time-to-Live (TTL) field in the IP datagram header?
A. Ensure packets reach destination within that time
B. Discard packets that reach later than that time
C. Prevent packets from looping indefinitely
D. Limit the time for which a packet queues at intermediate routers
questions.answer: C. Prevent packets from looping indefinitely
questions.to_find: The primary purpose of the TTL field in an IP datagram header

understand.plain: The TTL field in an IP header is a counter (hop count) that is decremented by each router that forwards the packet. If TTL reaches zero, the packet is discarded and an ICMP 'Time Exceeded' message is sent back. This prevents packets from circulating endlessly in routing loops.

keywords.explain: An 8-bit field in the IPv4 header. Each router decrements TTL by at least 1. When TTL = 0, the packet is discarded.
keywords.example: A packet with TTL=64 can traverse at most 64 routers before being dropped.

keywords.explain: A situation where routers forward a packet in a cycle, causing it to travel indefinitely without ever reaching the destination.
keywords.example: Router A -> Router B -> Router A -> ... If TTL were not decremented, this loop would last forever.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='460' height='140' viewBox='0 0 460 140'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='80' height='36' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='50' y='43' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>TTL=3</text><line x1='90' y1='38' x2='140' y2='38' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><defs><marker id='arr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='#7F77DD'/></marker></defs><rect x='140' y='20' width='80' height='36' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='180' y='43' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>TTL=2</text><line x1='220' y1='38' x2='270' y2='38' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><rect x='270' y='20' width='80' height='36' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='310' y='43' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>TTL=1</text><line x1='350' y1='38' x2='400' y2='38' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><rect x='400' y='20' width='50' height='36' rx='6' class='no'/><text x='425' y='43' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' style='fill:#fff'>DROP</text><text x='20' y='100' class='sub mut'>Each hop: TTL decremented by 1</text><text x='20' y='120' class='sub mut'>TTL = 0 at router  ->  packet discarded, ICMP sent back</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A packet with TTL=3 traverses three routers, each decrementing TTL by 1. When TTL reaches 0, the packet is dropped. This prevents infinite looping.

given.aim: Identify the correct reason IP includes the TTL field in the datagram header.

terms.meaning: An integer in the IP header (max 255) representing the maximum number of hops the packet may traverse.
terms.example: Linux sets default TTL = 64; Windows sets 128.
terms.connects: Each router decrements it; if it hits 0, the packet dies.

terms.meaning: Every router decrements TTL by at least 1 before forwarding.
terms.example: TTL=3 -> R1 -> TTL=2 -> R2 -> TTL=1 -> R3 -> TTL=0 -> DROP.
terms.connects: Guarantees the packet cannot live beyond a finite number of hops.

terms.meaning: Sent by a router back to the source when it discards a packet due to TTL=0.
terms.example: traceroute exploits this to discover the path to a host.
terms.connects: Informs the sender that the packet was discarded mid-route.

given.plan: TTL is a hop-count (not a real-time clock). It prevents routing loops by ensuring a packet is eventually discarded if it keeps circulating. Options A, B, D incorrectly describe it as a time-based mechanism; only C is correct.

steps.title: Analyze what TTL actually does
steps.apply: TTL is a hop counter, not a real-time clock. Each router subtracts 1. When TTL reaches 0, the packet is discarded and an ICMP 'Time Exceeded' is generated. This has nothing to do with wall-clock time.
steps.note: Despite the name 'Time-to-Live', TTL in modern IP is purely a hop count, not measured in seconds.

steps.title: Eliminate incorrect options
steps.apply: A: TTL doesn't guarantee delivery within a time limit. B: There is no mechanism to discard 'late' packets based on TTL. D: TTL does not control queuing time at routers. C: Routing loops are broken because every loop decrements TTL until it hits 0, at which point the packet is dropped.
steps.note: Option C precisely describes the real purpose: bounding the lifetime of a packet to prevent indefinite looping.

solution.result: IP uses TTL to prevent packets from looping indefinitely. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: IP TTL is a hop counter (max 255); every forwarding router decrements it by at least 1, discarding the packet when it reaches 0 to break routing loops.

questions.question:
One of the header fields in an IP datagram is the Time-to-Live (TTL) field. Which of the following statements best explains the need for this field?
A. It can be used to prioritize packets.
B. It can be used to reduce delays.
C. It can be used to optimize throughput.
D. It can be used to prevent packets from looping indefinitely.
questions.answer: D. It can be used to prevent packets from looping indefinitely.
questions.to_find: The best explanation for why the TTL field is needed in an IP datagram header

understand.plain: The TTL field is a hop-count field decremented by each router. When it reaches zero, the packet is dropped. This ensures that a packet caught in a routing loop will eventually be discarded rather than circulating forever, consuming network resources indefinitely.

keywords.explain: An 8-bit counter in the IPv4 header. Each router decrements it by 1 before forwarding; when it hits 0 the packet is discarded.
keywords.example: A packet with TTL=128 can pass through at most 128 routers.

keywords.explain: A network condition where packets are forwarded in a cycle between routers indefinitely due to inconsistent routing tables.
keywords.example: R1 routes to R2, R2 routes back to R1 — without TTL, the packet bounces forever.

understand.visual_alt: The TTL field acts as a safety valve: even if routing tables are inconsistent and create a loop, TTL ensures the packet is discarded after a bounded number of hops.

given.aim: Select the statement that best explains why the IP header includes a TTL field.

terms.meaning: Packets cycling through the same set of routers due to routing table errors.
terms.example: Distance-vector routing (like RIP) can temporarily create loops during convergence.
terms.connects: TTL is the mechanism that terminates such looping packets.

terms.meaning: Each router reduces TTL by at least 1 when it forwards a packet.
terms.example: TTL=5 -> router -> TTL=4 -> router -> ... -> TTL=0 -> DROP.
terms.connects: Guarantees finite lifetime for every packet.

terms.meaning: Without TTL, looping packets would consume bandwidth, CPU, and memory at every router they pass through.
terms.example: A flood of looping packets can saturate links, effectively a denial-of-service.
terms.connects: TTL prevents this by bounding packet lifetime.

given.plan: TTL is neither a QoS mechanism (priority), nor a performance optimizer (delay/throughput). Its sole design purpose is to give every packet a maximum hop count, preventing infinite loops.

steps.title: Evaluate option D against TTL's design purpose
steps.apply: In IP (RFC 791), the TTL field was specifically introduced to prevent packets from circulating forever in the internet. When a router forwards a packet it decrements TTL; TTL=0 -> discard. This directly prevents infinite looping.
steps.note: The original RFC 791 says the time is measured in seconds but in practice every router decrements by 1 per hop, making it effectively a hop counter.

steps.title: Eliminate options A, B, C
steps.apply: A (prioritization): IP uses the DSCP/ToS field, not TTL, for priority. B (reduce delays): TTL does not affect scheduling or queuing delays. C (optimize throughput): Throughput is determined by bandwidth and congestion control, not TTL. Only D correctly identifies the need for TTL.
steps.note: Options A, B, C describe functions of other IP header fields or higher-layer mechanisms, not TTL.

solution.result: TTL can be used to prevent packets from looping indefinitely. Answer: D.

questions.formula_note: IP TTL (RFC 791) is a hop-count limiter; its primary purpose is to bound packet lifetime and prevent routing loops from consuming network resources forever.

questions.question:
Host A (on TCP/IP v4 network A) sends an IP datagram D to host B (also on TCP/IP v4 network B). Assume that no error occurred during the transmission of D. When D reaches B, which of the following IP header field(s) may be different from that of the original datagram D sent by A?
i. TTL
ii. Checksum
iii. Fragment Offset

A. i only
B. ii and iii only
C. i and ii only
D. i, ii and iii
questions.to_find: Which IP header fields (TTL, Checksum, Fragment Offset) may have changed when the datagram arrives at the destination

understand.plain: As an IP datagram travels from source to destination, routers may modify certain header fields. TTL is decremented at every hop (so it will almost certainly change). When TTL changes, the checksum must be recomputed (so it changes too). If the datagram is larger than any intermediate link's MTU, routers fragment it, and Fragment Offset fields are set in the fragments — so Fragment Offset can also differ from the original.

keywords.explain: Each router subtracts 1 from TTL before forwarding. The TTL at B will be less than at A.
keywords.example: Source sends TTL=64; after 10 hops, destination receives TTL=54.

keywords.explain: The IPv4 header checksum covers all header fields. Whenever TTL changes, the checksum must be recalculated by the router.
keywords.example: If TTL was 64 and becomes 63, the checksum changes to reflect the new header state.

keywords.explain: If a datagram is larger than the MTU of an intermediate link, routers fragment it into smaller pieces. Each fragment gets a Fragment Offset indicating its position in the original datagram.
keywords.example: A 4000-byte datagram crossing an Ethernet link (MTU=1500) gets split; the fragments have Fragment Offset = 0, 185, 370 (in units of 8 bytes).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='500' height='180' viewBox='0 0 500 180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='100' height='50' rx='6' class='ok'/><text x='60' y='48' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' style='fill:#fff'>Host A</text><text x='60' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' style='fill:#fff'>TTL=64</text><line x1='110' y1='45' x2='190' y2='45' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#ar2)'/><defs><marker id='ar2' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='#7F77DD'/></marker></defs><rect x='190' y='10' width='120' height='70' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='250' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Router</text><text x='250' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>TTL--</text><text x='250' y='72' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Recompute cksum</text><text x='250' y='89' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Fragment if needed</text><line x1='310' y1='45' x2='390' y2='45' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#ar2)'/><rect x='390' y='20' width='100' height='50' rx='6' class='ok'/><text x='440' y='48' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' style='fill:#fff'>Host B</text><text x='440' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' style='fill:#fff'>TTL changed</text><text x='10' y='130' class='sub mut'>Fields that MAY change: TTL (always), Checksum (due to TTL change), Fragment Offset (if fragmented)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: An IP datagram travels from Host A through routers to Host B. Each router decrements TTL and recomputes the checksum. If fragmentation occurs at any router, the Fragment Offset fields in the resulting fragments differ from the original. All three fields (TTL, Checksum, Fragment Offset) can be different at the destination.

given.aim: Determine which IP header fields (TTL, Checksum, Fragment Offset) could have different values at the destination compared to the original datagram sent by the source.

terms.meaning: 8-bit hop count; decremented by every forwarding router
terms.example: Source TTL=64; after 5 hops -> TTL=59 at destination.
terms.connects: Will definitely be different from original unless the path has 0 hops (same host), which is not the case here.

terms.meaning: 16-bit one's complement of all 16-bit words in the IP header
terms.example: Any change to TTL requires the router to recalculate the header checksum.
terms.connects: Changes whenever any header field (at minimum TTL) changes.

terms.meaning: 13-bit field indicating the offset (in units of 8 bytes) of this fragment's data relative to the original unfragmented datagram
terms.example: Original datagram has Fragment Offset=0. After fragmentation, subsequent pieces have offsets 185, 370, etc.
terms.connects: Can change if any intermediate router fragments the datagram (which is allowed in IPv4 even without an error).

given.plan: TTL is always decremented -> always changes. Checksum covers TTL -> changes whenever TTL changes. Fragmentation is legal in IPv4 at any router (no error constraint violated) -> Fragment Offset can change. So all three fields (i, ii, iii) may differ.

steps.title: Analyze TTL
steps.apply: Host A and Host B are on different networks (network A and network B), so there is at least one router between them. Every router decrements TTL by 1. Therefore TTL at B is at least 1 less than TTL sent by A. Field i (TTL) CHANGES.
steps.note: TTL always changes for inter-network communication since at least one router is involved.

steps.title: Analyze Checksum
steps.formula_raw: new_checksum = ones_complement_sum(all 16-bit header words with updated TTL)
steps.apply: The IPv4 header checksum is computed over the entire header including the TTL field. When TTL is decremented, the checksum MUST be recomputed. So checksum at B differs from the original. Field ii (Checksum) CHANGES.
steps.note: Routers must update the checksum whenever they modify any header field (which includes TTL decrementation).

steps.title: Analyze Fragment Offset
steps.formula_raw: Fragment Offset = (byte offset of fragment data) / 8
steps.apply: IPv4 allows routers to fragment datagrams when the packet size exceeds the link MTU. The problem states 'no error occurred' but fragmentation is not an error — it is a normal IP operation. If an intermediate router fragments D, the created fragments carry Fragment Offset values (0 for first, and non-zero for subsequent). These differ from the original datagram's Fragment Offset. Field iii (Fragment Offset) MAY CHANGE.
steps.note: The question asks which fields 'may be different', not 'will always be different'. Since fragmentation is possible (not an error), Fragment Offset is a valid answer.

solution.result: All three fields — TTL (i), Checksum (ii), and Fragment Offset (iii) — may be different when the datagram reaches Host B. Answer: D. i, ii and iii.

questions.formula_note: IPv4 header fields modified in transit: TTL decremented per hop; Checksum recomputed when TTL changes; Fragment Offset set by routers upon fragmentation. All three are mutable during normal (error-free) IP forwarding.
